</security_assessment>

<details>

LLM auditresponse for yed.install:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The file `yed.install` contains standard ALPM (Arch Linux Package Management) install script hooks. `post_install` and `post_upgrade` only print a reminder about accepting the upstream license agreement. The line `op=$1; shift; $op $*` is a common pattern used by pacman to dispatch the appropriate hook based on the first argument (e.g., `post_install`). Since the arguments are provided by pacman itself (which uses a fixed set of well‑known hook names), this does **not** introduce an arbitrary code execution vulnerability. There is no obfuscated code, no network requests, no file operations beyond the intended message output, and no deviation from standard packaging practices. The behavior is consistent with the package’s stated purpose and presents no security threat.
</details>
<summary>Standard install hooks with no malicious behavior.</summary>
</security_assessment>

audits.body:
Cloning https://aur.archlinux.org/yed.git to /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-5kuhlm1_...

Analyzing yed AUR package (working in /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-5kuhlm1_) with qwen/qwen3-235b-a22b-2507 from https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines standard sources, including a direct HTTPS download of a versioned ZIP archive from the official yWorks website, along with local file copies (desktop entry, launcher script, MIME definition). All `sha256sums` are explicitly listed and match the sources, so no 'SKIP' is used — checksums will be verified during source fetch. The package version is pinned (`pkgver=3.25.1`), and there is no `pkgver()` function that could execute arbitrary code during sourcing. The top-level scope contains only static declarations; no command substitution or immediate execution occurs outside of these definitions.

No dangerous operations (e.g., `curl | bash`, `eval`, base64-decoding of remote content, or data exfiltration) are present. The `source` array uses only trusted, direct URLs associated with the official vendor. Since `--noprepare` and `--nobuild` prevent execution of `package()` and other functions, no installation logic will run at this stage. Sourcing the PKGBUILD for `makepkg --printsrcinfo` and source fetching is therefore safe.

</details>
<summary>
Safe sources, no dynamic code in pkgver or top-level</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- Safe sources, no dynamic code in pkgver or top-level[/]
Running makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare to download sources for agent...

LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD is for the yEd graph editor, a legitimate Java application distributed by yWorks. The package retrieves its source from the official yWorks resource URL (`https://www.yworks.com/resources/yed/demo/`) using a versioned zip file, which is standard practice for official releases. All source files are verified with matching sha256 checksums, indicating strong integrity controls.

The `package()` function performs standard, safe operations: installing JAR files, libraries, licenses, icons, MIME definitions, desktop entries, and a launcher script into appropriate system directories. The icon installation loop uses `ls` and `sed` to extract dimensions from filenames (e.g., `yed16.png`, `yed32.png`)—a slightly unconventional but harmless method to populate hicolor icon sizes.

No network operations, dynamic downloads, or code execution occur during packaging. No obfuscation, suspicious scripts, or unusual commands (e.g., `curl|bash`, `eval`, `base64`) are present. The package depends only on standard Java runtime and icon theme packages. The license is marked as 'custom', which is expected for proprietary software like yEd.

This PKGBUILD adheres to standard AUR packaging practices and poses no supply-chain threat.
</details>
<summary>
Standard, safe packaging of official Java application
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[1/3] Reviewing graphml+xml-mime.xml, yed.install...
+ Reviewed PKGBUILD. Status: SAFE -- Standard, safe packaging of official Java application
LLM auditresponse for graphml+xml-mime.xml:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided file is a standard MIME type definition XML file used to register the `application/x-graphml+xml` MIME type for yEd graphml files. It follows the freedesktop.org shared-mime-info standard and contains only declarative data: a comment, a file extension glob pattern (`*.graphml`), and a generic icon name. There is no executable code, no network activity, no file manipulation commands, and no obfuscated content.

This file is intended to be installed into the system's MIME database (typically under `/usr/share/mime/`) so that desktop environments can correctly associate `.graphml` files with the yEd application. Such XML files are a normal part of desktop integration for applications that handle custom file types. The content is static, human-readable, and poses no security risk.

The use of XML entity escaping (e.g., ", ') is correct and consistent with XML syntax. This is not obfuscation but proper formatting. There are no signs of a supply-chain attack, backdoor, or malicious behavior. The file serves a legitimate and benign packaging purpose.
</details>
<summary>
Standard MIME definition file, no executable content
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[2/3] Reviewing yed.install...
+ Reviewed graphml+xml-mime.xml. Status: SAFE -- Standard MIME definition file, no executable content
LLM auditresponse for yed.install:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The `yed.install` script is a standard Arch Linux package install script that defines `post_upgrade` and `post_install` functions. These functions are executed automatically by pacman during package installation or upgrade. The script outputs a message reminding users to accept the license agreement located at `/usr/share/licenses/yed/license.html`, which is a legitimate and common practice for proprietary or license-restricted software.

The final lines of the script (`op=$1; shift; $op $*`) dynamically invoke the appropriate function based on the argument passed by pacman (e.g., `post_install`, `post_upgrade`). This pattern is a conventional idiom in .install scripts and does not introduce any unsafe behavior, as the functions called are predefined and the arguments are controlled by pacman. There are no network operations, file modifications outside expected package management behavior, or execution of external code.

While the dynamic function dispatch might appear unusual at first glance, it is a known and accepted pattern in AUR and official packages. The script does not exfiltrate data, execute untrusted code, or perform any unauthorized system changes. It only displays license-related information to the user.
</details>
<summary>
Standard install script; only displays license notice
</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for yed.vmoptions:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided file `yed.vmoptions` contains a list of JVM command-line options using `--add-opens` to open specific internal Java modules and packages to unnamed modules. This is a common requirement for Java applications that use reflection or depend on internal Swing or XML parser components, especially when running on Java 9 or later with strong encapsulation by default.

These flags are typically needed by desktop applications like Yed (a graph editor) that rely on deep integration with Java's GUI toolkits (Swing) and may access non-public APIs for customization or performance. The use of `ALL-UNNAMED` allows all unnamed modules (such as the application's own classes) to access the specified internal packages. This is standard practice in many Java desktop apps distributed via AUR or other packaging systems.

There is no executable code, network activity, obfuscation, or file system modifications in this file. It is a configuration file intended solely for JVM tuning and does not introduce any malicious behavior. The file aligns with expected packaging practices for Java-based applications on Arch Linux.
</details>
<summary>
Configuration file for JVM; no malicious content</summary>
</security_assessment>

[1/5] Reviewing yed, yed.desktop, src/yed-3.25.1/license.html, src/yed-3.25.1/thirdpartylicenses.html...
+ Reviewed src/yed-3.25.1/yed.vmoptions. Status: SAFE -- Configuration file for JVM; no malicious content
LLM auditresponse for yed:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided file is a simple wrapper script for launching the yEd graph editor, a Java-based application. It uses `archlinux-java-run` to execute the yEd JAR file located at `/usr/share/java/yed/yed.jar` with the specified Java runtime (`-b 25`) and forwards all command-line arguments (`"$@"`) to the application.

The script follows standard AUR packaging practices for Java applications. It does not perform any network operations, file downloads, or system modifications. There is no use of dangerous commands like `eval`, `curl`, `wget`, or obfuscated code. The use of `"$@"` is a correctly quoted and escaped way to pass through command-line arguments, even though it appears in a mangled form due to HTML escaping in the source—it likely originates from `"$@"` in the real script.

The behavior is consistent with a legitimate launcher script and poses no evidence of malicious supply-chain modifications.
</details>
<summary>
Standard Java application launcher, no malicious activity detected</summary>
</security_assessment>

[2/5] Reviewing yed.desktop, src/yed-3.25.1/license.html, src/yed-3.25.1/thirdpartylicenses.html...

LLM auditresponse for license.html:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided file is an HTML-formatted license agreement for the yEd software, distributed by yWorks GmbH. It outlines standard licensing terms, including permitted usage, disclaimers of warranty, limitations of liability, and distribution restrictions. The content is consistent with a legitimate software license and does not contain executable code, scripts, or any form of malicious content.

Several sections mention integration with third-party libraries (e.g., Apache Batik, Apache POI, ANTLR, TwelveMonkeys), along with their respective open-source licenses. These references are typical in software license disclosures and include only publicly available, well-known projects. All hyperlinks point to official project and license pages (e.g., apache.org, github.com/haraldk/TwelveMonkeys), which are legitimate and expected sources. There is no obfuscation, code injection, or network exfiltration mechanism present.

The file serves an informational purpose and poses no security risk. It does not perform any system operations, downloads, or data transmissions. As such, it aligns with standard packaging practices for disclosing dependencies and licensing terms in software distributions, including those on the AUR.
</details>
<summary>
License file with third-party attributions, no malicious content
</summary>
</security_assessment>

The provided `yed.desktop` file is a standard desktop entry configuration used to integrate the yEd Graph Editor application into desktop environments on Linux systems. It specifies metadata such as the application name, icon, execution command, supported MIME types, and categories for menu organization. All fields follow the expected format defined by the freedesktop.org Desktop Entry Specification.

There are no executable scripts, network operations, file manipulations, or any form of code within this file. The `Exec=yed %f` line is standard practice for launching the application with an optional file argument. The `StartupWMClass` and `MimeType` entries are used for desktop environment integration and are benign. No suspicious or obfuscated content is present.

This file serves only to register the application in the system's application menu and handle file associations. It does not perform any actions by itself and poses no security risk. The content aligns with typical desktop entry files distributed with Java-based or native applications on Linux.
